name: Methionine and cysteine metabolism (methionine cycle & transsulfuration)
summary: Methionine is activated to the universal methyl donor S-adenosylmethionine; the homocysteine that remains is either remethylated back to methionine or committed through cystathionine to cysteine, feeding glutathione, taurine, and the gasotransmitter hydrogen sulfide.
description: This module links two coupled cycles of sulfur-amino-acid metabolism. **(1) The methionine cycle:** methionine adenosyltransferase (MAT) condenses methionine with ATP to make S-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAM/AdoMet), the methyl donor for hundreds of SAM-dependent methyltransferases (represented here by the quantitatively dominant hepatic enzyme glycine N-methyltransferase, GNMT). Every transmethylation yields S-adenosyl-L-homocysteine (SAH/AdoHcy), which adenosylhomocysteinase (AHCY) hydrolyzes to homocysteine + adenosine. Homocysteine is remethylated to methionine either by the vitamin-B12-dependent methionine synthase (MTR), using 5-methyltetrahydrofolate donated by the folate cycle, or by betaine–homocysteine methyltransferase (BHMT) in liver and kidney. **(2) The transsulfuration pathway:** when methionine is in excess, SAM allosterically activates cystathionine β-synthase (CBS), which condenses homocysteine with serine to cystathionine; cystathionine γ-lyase (CTH/CSE) then cleaves cystathionine to cysteine, 2-oxobutanoate, and ammonia. This is the only route by which the sulfur of methionine reaches cysteine, and hence glutathione and taurine. **(3) Hydrogen-sulfide biogenesis:** CBS, CSE, and 3-mercaptopyruvate sulfurtransferase (MPST/3-MST, fed by cysteine aminotransferase) also run alternative desulfhydration reactions that release H2S, a redox-active gasotransmitter. The SAM:SAH ratio, together with SAM's opposite effects on CBS (activation) and methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR, inhibition), acts as the master switch that partitions homocysteine between conservation (remethylation) and disposal (transsulfuration).

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 18 (amino acid oxidation & biosynthesis)

references.title: S-adenosylmethionine: a control switch that regulates liver function
Relative contributions of cystathionine beta-synthase and gamma-cystathionase to H2S biogenesis via alternative trans-sulfuration reactions
H2S biogenesis by human cystathionine gamma-lyase leads to the novel sulfur metabolites lanthionine and homolanthionine and is responsive to the grade of hyperhomocysteinemia
Glycine N-methyltransferase and regulation of S-adenosylmethionine levels

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Principal site of the methionine cycle and transsulfuration reactions.

compartments.name: Mitochondrion
compartments.description: CBS, CSE and MPST also maintain mitochondrial pools that contribute to compartmentalized H2S production.

metabolites.name: S-Adenosyl-L-methionine (SAM / AdoMet)
metabolites.description: Universal methyl donor and allosteric effector; the master regulatory metabolite of this module.

metabolites.name: S-Adenosyl-L-homocysteine (SAH / AdoHcy)
metabolites.description: Product and competitive inhibitor of SAM-dependent methyltransferases; the SAM:SAH ratio sets cellular methylation capacity.

metabolites.name: L-Homocysteine
metabolites.description: Branch-point thiol partitioned between remethylation and transsulfuration; elevated levels (hyperhomocysteinemia) are a cardiovascular risk marker.

metabolites.name: 5-Methyltetrahydrofolate
metabolites.description: One-carbon donor for B12-dependent homocysteine remethylation; the junction with folate/one-carbon metabolism.

metabolites.name: Betaine (glycine betaine)
metabolites.description: Alternative methyl donor for homocysteine remethylation by BHMT; also a compatible osmolyte.

metabolites.name: L-Cystathionine
metabolites.description: Committed transsulfuration intermediate; accumulates when CTH/CSE is deficient or vitamin-B6 limited.

metabolites.name: L-Cysteine
metabolites.description: Transsulfuration end product and rate-limiting precursor of glutathione, taurine, and H2S.

metabolites.name: 2-Oxobutanoate (alpha-ketobutyrate)
metabolites.description: Carbon skeleton released by CTH; oxidatively decarboxylated to propionyl-CoA and funneled into the TCA cycle as succinyl-CoA.

metabolites.name: Diphosphate (PPi)
metabolites.description: Released by MAT's unusual tripolyphosphate cleavage of ATP; its subsequent hydrolysis by pyrophosphatase pulls SAM synthesis forward.

metabolites.name: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S)
metabolites.description: Endogenous gasotransmitter produced by CBS, CSE and 3-MST; signals through protein persulfidation and modulates vascular tone and mitochondrial bioenergetics.

metabolites.name: 3-Mercaptopyruvate
metabolites.description: Sulfur-bearing substrate of MPST/3-MST; generated from cysteine by cysteine aminotransferase.

metabolites.name: Thioredoxin (reduced / dithiol)
metabolites.description: Physiological reductant that liberates H2S from the MPST-bound persulfide.

enzymes.name: Methionine adenosyltransferase 1A (MAT I / MAT III)
enzymes.description: Liver-specific isozyme; uniquely activated (rather than inhibited) by its product SAM, enabling hepatic clearance of methionine loads.

enzymes.name: Methionine adenosyltransferase 2A (MAT II)
enzymes.description: Widely expressed extrahepatic isozyme; feedback-inhibited by SAM and up-regulated in proliferating and tumor cells.

enzymes.name: Glycine N-methyltransferase
enzymes.description: Most abundant hepatic methyltransferase; buffers the SAM:SAH ratio by disposing of surplus SAM as sarcosine. Representative of the many SAM-dependent methyltransferases that generate SAH.

enzymes.name: Adenosylhomocysteinase (SAH hydrolase)
enzymes.description: Uses a tightly bound NAD+ as a catalytic (non-consumed) cofactor. EC reclassified from 3.3.1.1 to 3.13.2.1.

enzymes.name: Methionine synthase (5-methyltetrahydrofolate–homocysteine methyltransferase)
enzymes.description: Cobalamin-dependent; couples the folate cycle to methionine regeneration. Its cob(I)alamin is periodically reactivated by methionine synthase reductase (MTRR).

enzymes.name: Betaine—homocysteine S-methyltransferase 1
enzymes.description: Folate/B12-independent remethylation route confined mainly to liver and kidney.

enzymes.name: Cystathionine beta-synthase
enzymes.description: Committed, rate-controlling step of transsulfuration. Allosterically activated by SAM via its C-terminal Bateman (CBS) domain; its regulatory heme senses CO and NO, linking the three gasotransmitters. Loss-of-function mutations cause classical homocystinuria.

enzymes.name: Cystathionine gamma-lyase (CSE)
enzymes.description: PLP-dependent lyase that releases cysteine; also the dominant H2S-generating enzyme of the cardiovascular system via promiscuous desulfhydration of cysteine and homocysteine.

enzymes.name: Cysteine aminotransferase / aspartate aminotransferase, cytoplasmic
enzymes.description: The cytoplasmic aspartate aminotransferase moonlights as cysteine aminotransferase, transaminating cysteine to 3-mercaptopyruvate to feed the MPST/3-MST route to H2S.

enzymes.name: 3-Mercaptopyruvate sulfurtransferase (3-MST)
enzymes.description: Third H2S-generating enzyme; forms an enzyme-bound persulfide from 3-mercaptopyruvate that releases H2S upon reduction by thioredoxin or other thiols. Prominent in mitochondria.

enzymes.name: Methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (NADPH)
enzymes.description: Produces the 5-methyltetrahydrofolate consumed by methionine synthase; its catalytic step belongs to folate/one-carbon metabolism (crosstalk). Included here as the target of SAM allosteric inhibition — the counterweight to SAM's activation of CBS. The common c.677C>T (A222V) variant lowers activity and raises homocysteine.

reactions.name: Methionine activation (SAM synthesis)
reactions.equation: L-Methionine + ATP + H2O -> S-Adenosyl-L-methionine + Orthophosphate + Diphosphate

provenance.notes: Equilibrium favors SAH synthesis; driven toward hydrolysis in vivo by continual removal of adenosine and homocysteine.

reactions.name: H2S generation by CBS (cysteine + homocysteine condensation)

provenance.notes: Alternative beta-replacement in which cysteine's thiol, rather than serine's hydroxyl, is the leaving group; identified as the dominant CBS route to H2S. No dedicated KEGG reaction id, so the KEGG xref is intentionally omitted.

reactions.name: H2S generation by CSE (cysteine desulfhydration)
reactions.equation: L-Cysteine + H2O -> Pyruvate + Ammonia + Hydrogen sulfide

provenance.notes: Promiscuous alpha,beta-elimination activity of cystathionine gamma-lyase; CSE is the principal vascular H2S source. CSE additionally forms lanthionine/homolanthionine from cysteine and homocysteine.
Cysteine aminotransferase activity of GOT1/GOT2; supplies 3-mercaptopyruvate for the 3-MST branch of H2S production.

reactions.name: H2S generation by 3-mercaptopyruvate sulfurtransferase (3-MST)
reactions.equation: 3-Mercaptopyruvate + Thioredoxin (reduced) -> Pyruvate + Hydrogen sulfide + Thioredoxin disulfide

provenance.notes: Net reaction; mechanistically proceeds via an enzyme cysteine-persulfide intermediate whose sulfane sulfur is released as H2S upon reduction by thioredoxin (or other cellular thiols).

regulations.mechanism: SAM binds the C-terminal regulatory (Bateman/CBS) domain of cystathionine beta-synthase and relieves autoinhibition, accelerating transsulfuration. This diverts homocysteine toward cysteine/H2S synthesis precisely when methionine (and therefore SAM) is abundant.

SAM allosterically inhibits methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase, throttling 5-methyl-THF supply and slowing homocysteine remethylation. Together with its activation of CBS, this makes SAM a reciprocal switch that shifts flux from conservation (remethylation) to disposal (transsulfuration).

SAH is a competitive product inhibitor of SAM-dependent methyltransferases; a falling SAM:SAH ratio suppresses transmethylation. GNMT is comparatively resistant, which lets it act as the SAM overflow valve while other methyltransferases are already shut down.

5-Methyltetrahydrofolate is a tight-binding inhibitor of glycine N-methyltransferase. When folate/one-carbon supply is high (favoring remethylation), it restrains GNMT-mediated SAM catabolism, integrating folate status with methyl-group homeostasis.
Unusually, the hepatic MAT I/III isozyme is activated by its own product SAM, giving the liver the capacity to dispose of large postprandial methionine loads.

The extrahepatic MAT II isozyme is feedback-inhibited by SAM, tightly limiting SAM levels in non-hepatic tissues — a contrast to hepatic MAT I/III that distinguishes proliferative from quiescent methionine handling.

relations.note: MAT1A (hepatic MAT I/III) and MAT2A (extrahepatic MAT II) are isozymes of the same reaction with opposite SAM regulation and a well-known MAT1A-to-MAT2A switch in liver injury and hepatocarcinogenesis.
The folate/one-carbon cycle supplies 5-methyl-THF for B12-dependent homocysteine remethylation; MTHFR is the shared junction and SAM's inhibition of MTHFR is where the two pathways interlock.
Transsulfuration-derived cysteine is the rate-limiting precursor of glutathione, linking sulfur-amino-acid supply to cellular antioxidant capacity.
Cysteine is oxidized by cysteine dioxygenase and decarboxylated toward hypotaurine and taurine.
2-Oxobutanoate from CTH is oxidatively decarboxylated to propionyl-CoA and enters the TCA cycle as succinyl-CoA (anaplerosis), disposing of the methionine carbon skeleton.
The serine consumed by CBS is drawn from serine biosynthesis, which branches off the glycolytic intermediate 3-phosphoglycerate.
Adenosine released by AHCY is salvaged or deaminated by purine metabolism; its efficient removal is what pulls the reversible SAH hydrolase reaction toward homocysteine.
Decarboxylated SAM donates aminopropyl groups for spermidine/spermine synthesis, an alternative SAM-consuming route that also feeds back into the methionine salvage pathway.
